A clean tokenizer should preserve ordinary English punctuation and spacing.
The small pilot corpus must be reproducible before any larger experiment begins.
Source approval is a gate, not a formality.
The report should separate observations from recommendations.
A validation script should fail loudly when a required field is missing.
The model cannot rely on instruction tuning to repair a broken base checkpoint.
Every source needs a license URL, attribution notes, and redistribution notes.
A checksum does not replace a license review, but it helps make retrieval reproducible.
The first candidate may be smaller if it passes the quality gates with lower cost.
Do not train on malformed fixtures; they exist only to test rejection behavior.
The phrase `target_total` should remain distinct from `max_new` in command-line tools.
A path like C:\\tmp\\sample.jsonl should round-trip without dropping backslashes.
The URL https://example.invalid/data should remain a single readable span.
Numbers such as 45M, 60M, 12k, 16k, and 24k appear often in design notes.
Quoted text, commas, semicolons; and parentheses (like this) need stable segmentation.
A small model benefits when the tokenizer avoids wasting parameters on corrupted forms.
If a source is too large for the pilot cap, defer it instead of stretching the rule.
The manifest hash should be stable even when timestamps differ.
A candidate can be rejected for one hard failure even if its compression looks strong.
License ambiguity is not a warning for this gate; it is a failure.
The base model should produce readable fragments before any instruction dataset is used.
A vocabulary of 24,000 tokens costs more than 16,000 tokens at the same embedding size.
The audit should print both accepted and rejected document counts.
Round-trip checks catch tokenizers that silently normalize away important characters.
The same config should serialize deterministically on every test run.
A rejected dataset can still be mentioned in documentation as a risk example.
Short sentences help test common words, but they cannot replace paragraph samples.
The output should not mix corrupted byte sequences into ordinary Latin text.
A human reviewer should be able to trace every corpus line back to its source family.
The registry intentionally stores approval reasons instead of implicit assumptions.
A tiny smoke test is useful only if its inputs are cleaner than the failed baseline.
Parameter savings from tied embeddings should be recorded before architecture choices are made.
If a validator cannot prove safety, it should return FAIL rather than PASS with a caveat.
The data plan is allowed to be conservative; it is not allowed to be vague.
The source review should explain why a tempting dataset was deferred or rejected.
A candidate tokenizer must preserve special tokens exactly as configured.
This sentence includes apostrophes: the model's tokenizer shouldn't split them oddly.
A deterministic split seed prevents accidental train/test drift.
The phrase end-of-sequence is different from the literal token </s>.
Documentation should be precise enough that the next phase can stop safely.
The quick brown fox sentence is common, but this suite needs domain-specific examples too.
Tables, lists, and headings should be normalized before they become tokenizer input.
A model card should not claim capabilities that evaluation has not demonstrated.
Corpus hygiene is easier to preserve when every rejection reason is counted.
The tokenizer pilot should be boring in the best possible way: repeatable and inspectable.
A download cap protects both storage and experimental discipline.
The validator should reject a source that says commercial use is unknown.
A single source should not dominate the tokenizer just because it is easy to fetch.
Clean English samples should include plain prose, commands, filenames, and URLs.
The next phase can add a fetcher, but only after source approval is explicit.
A sentence with 3.14159 and 1,024 checks numeric punctuation.
The field name `official_license_url` should not be confused with a homepage.
Some sources are useful later but still outside the current pilot boundary.
The safest default is to require proof instead of relying on a dataset nickname.
No generated teacher data is needed for tokenizer selection.
The training script should stay idle until the base pipeline passes its own gates.
A clean failure report is more valuable than a silent partial success.
The phrase mixed-script corruption should remain readable after encoding and decoding.
The README should state what the phase does not do as clearly as what it adds.
Before scaling up, the team should know exactly why the pilot tokenizer won.
